Back: The capacity of one system to do (or be able to do) work on another system.
The energy an object possesses due to its motion; the work needed to accelerate it from rest to its current speed.
Energy stored within a physical system due to position or configuration.
The amount of energy transferred by a force acting over a distance.
The total energy of an isolated system remains constant, although it may change forms.
The rate at which work is performed or energy is transferred (energy per unit time).
The SI unit of energy.
The SI unit of power, equal to one joule per second.
A force that does zero net work on a particle that travels along any closed path.
The factor by which a mechanism multiplies the input force.
A unit of energy equal to the kinetic energy gained by one electron accelerated through a potential difference of one volt.
A quantum phenomenon in which electrons are emitted from matter after absorbing energy from electromagnetic radiation.
Source: WikiPremed (CC BY-NC-SA) - Modern Physics & Atomic Theory - https://www.wikipremed.com/mcat_course_code-010601.html

Back: The minimum energy needed to remove an electron from a solid to a point just outside its surface.
Source: WikiPremed (CC BY-NC-SA) - Modern Physics & Atomic Theory - https://www.wikipremed.com/mcat_course_code-010601.html

Back: A physical constant that sets the scale of quanta and relates a photon's energy to its frequency; central to quantum mechanics.
Source: WikiPremed (CC BY-NC-SA) - Modern Physics & Atomic Theory - https://www.wikipremed.com/mcat_course_code-010601.html

Back: The statement that all matter has a wave-like nature (wave-particle duality).
Source: WikiPremed (CC BY-NC-SA) - Modern Physics & Atomic Theory - https://www.wikipremed.com/mcat_course_code-010601.html

Back: There is a lower bound on the product of the uncertainties in a particle's position and momentum; both cannot be arbitrarily well-defined at once.
Source: WikiPremed (CC BY-NC-SA) - Modern Physics & Atomic Theory - https://www.wikipremed.com/mcat_course_code-010601.html

Back: The decrease in energy (increase in wavelength) of an X-ray or gamma-ray photon when it scatters off matter.
Source: WikiPremed (CC BY-NC-SA) - Modern Physics & Atomic Theory - https://www.wikipremed.com/mcat_course_code-010601.html

Back: The concept that any mass has an associated energy and vice versa (E = mc^2).
Source: WikiPremed (CC BY-NC-SA) - Modern Physics & Atomic Theory - https://www.wikipremed.com/mcat_course_code-010601.html

Back: Describes the spectral radiance of electromagnetic radiation emitted by a blackbody at a given temperature.
Source: WikiPremed (CC BY-NC-SA) - Modern Physics & Atomic Theory - https://www.wikipremed.com/mcat_course_code-010601.html

Back: A technique that studies matter by firing electrons at a sample and observing the resulting interference pattern (evidence of electron wave nature).
Source: WikiPremed (CC BY-NC-SA) - Modern Physics & Atomic Theory - https://www.wikipremed.com/mcat_course_code-010601.html

Back: The branch of physics based on quantization, begun in 1900 when Planck explained the emission spectrum of black bodies.
Source: WikiPremed (CC BY-NC-SA) - Modern Physics & Atomic Theory - https://www.wikipremed.com/mcat_course_code-010601.html

Back: A compound that, dissolved in water, gives a solution with pH below 7; more generally, a proton donor.
A substance that can accept protons.
A measure of the acidity or alkalinity of a solution.
An acid that dissociates completely in aqueous solution.
An acid that does not ionize in solution to a significant extent.
A base that does not ionize fully in aqueous solution.
A species that can accept a pair of electrons to form a coordinate covalent bond.
A species that can donate a pair of electrons to form a coordinate covalent bond.
The equilibrium constant for the dissociation of a weak acid.
A solution that resists changes in pH upon addition of small amounts of acid or base, or upon dilution.
A volumetric method to determine the concentration of an unknown acid or base via a neutralization reaction.
The point in a titration where the amount of titrant added equals the amount of analyte present.
A reaction in which an acid and a base react to produce a salt and water.
The cation (H3O+) derived from protonation of water.
A molecule containing both amine and carboxyl functional groups; the building block of proteins.
Source: WikiPremed (CC BY-NC-SA) - Amino Acids & Protein Structure - https://www.wikipremed.com/mcat_course_code-040101.html

Back: The bond formed when the carboxyl group of one amino acid reacts with the amino group of another, releasing water.
Source: WikiPremed (CC BY-NC-SA) - Amino Acids & Protein Structure - https://www.wikipremed.com/mcat_course_code-040101.html

Back: A large polymer of amino acids joined in a linear chain by peptide bonds.
Source: WikiPremed (CC BY-NC-SA) - Amino Acids & Protein Structure - https://www.wikipremed.com/mcat_course_code-040101.html

Back: The exact amino-acid sequence of a protein, including its atoms, bonds, and stereochemistry.
Source: WikiPremed (CC BY-NC-SA) - Amino Acids & Protein Structure - https://www.wikipremed.com/mcat_course_code-040101.html

Back: The local three-dimensional forms of segments of a protein, such as alpha helices and beta sheets.
Source: WikiPremed (CC BY-NC-SA) - Amino Acids & Protein Structure - https://www.wikipremed.com/mcat_course_code-040101.html

Back: The overall three-dimensional structure of a single protein molecule.
Source: WikiPremed (CC BY-NC-SA) - Amino Acids & Protein Structure - https://www.wikipremed.com/mcat_course_code-040101.html

Back: The arrangement of multiple folded protein subunits in a multi-subunit complex.
Source: WikiPremed (CC BY-NC-SA) - Amino Acids & Protein Structure - https://www.wikipremed.com/mcat_course_code-040101.html

Back: A common secondary-structure motif: a right-handed coil in which each backbone N-H hydrogen-bonds to the carbonyl four residues earlier.
Source: WikiPremed (CC BY-NC-SA) - Amino Acids & Protein Structure - https://www.wikipremed.com/mcat_course_code-040101.html

Back: A secondary structure of strands connected laterally by hydrogen bonds into a pleated sheet.
Source: WikiPremed (CC BY-NC-SA) - Amino Acids & Protein Structure - https://www.wikipremed.com/mcat_course_code-040101.html

Back: The region of an enzyme that contains the catalytic and binding sites.
Source: WikiPremed (CC BY-NC-SA) - Amino Acids & Protein Structure - https://www.wikipremed.com/mcat_course_code-040101.html

Back: Enzyme inhibition in which the inhibitor binds the active site and prevents substrate binding (apparent Km rises; Vmax unchanged).
Source: WikiPremed (CC BY-NC-SA) - Amino Acids & Protein Structure - https://www.wikipremed.com/mcat_course_code-040101.html

Back: Inhibition that lowers the maximum rate (Vmax) without changing the substrate's apparent binding affinity (Km).
Source: WikiPremed (CC BY-NC-SA) - Amino Acids & Protein Structure - https://www.wikipremed.com/mcat_course_code-040101.html

Back: The pH at which a molecule carries no net electrical charge.
Source: WikiPremed (CC BY-NC-SA) - Amino Acids & Protein Structure - https://www.wikipremed.com/mcat_course_code-040101.html

Back: A molecule that is electrically neutral overall but carries both positive and negative formal charges on different atoms.
Source: WikiPremed (CC BY-NC-SA) - Amino Acids & Protein Structure - https://www.wikipremed.com/mcat_course_code-040101.html

Back: A covalent bond formed by coupling two thiol groups; stabilizes protein tertiary structure.
Source: WikiPremed (CC BY-NC-SA) - Amino Acids & Protein Structure - https://www.wikipremed.com/mcat_course_code-040101.html

Back: A nucleic acid that contains the genetic instructions used in the development and functioning of living organisms.
A nucleic acid polymer of nucleotides that helps translate genetic information from DNA into proteins.
A compound consisting of a nitrogenous base, a sugar, and one or more phosphate groups.
A nitrogenous base attached to a ribose or deoxyribose sugar (no phosphate group).
Two complementary nucleotides on opposite nucleic-acid strands joined by hydrogen bonds.
A double-ring nitrogenous base (adenine and guanine).
A single-ring nitrogenous base (cytosine, thymine, and uracil).
An RNA molecule encoding the chemical blueprint for a protein product.
A small RNA that delivers a specific amino acid to the ribosome during translation.
RNA synthesized in the nucleolus that is the central component of the ribosome.
A nucleotide that serves as the main molecular currency of intracellular energy transfer.
The complex of DNA and protein that makes up chromosomes.
A chief protein component of chromatin, acting as a spool around which DNA winds and playing a role in gene regulation.
The property of double-stranded nucleic acids in which base pairs form between strands via hydrogen bonds.
An electrically excitable cell that processes and transmits information in the nervous system.
The largest part of the nervous system, comprising the brain and the spinal cord.
The part of the nervous system outside the brain and spinal cord, serving the limbs and organs.
A spike of electrical discharge that travels along the membrane of a cell.
The membrane potential of a cell when there are no action potentials or other active changes.
The electrical voltage across a cell's plasma membrane.
A decrease in the absolute value of a cell's membrane potential.
The branch of the autonomic nervous system that becomes more active during stress (fight-or-flight).
The autonomic branch that, in opposition to the sympathetic system, governs rest-and-digest functions.
The peripheral branch associated with voluntary control of body movement and reception of external stimuli.
The peripheral branch that acts as a control system maintaining homeostasis (involuntary).
A chemical used to relay, amplify, and modulate signals between a neuron and another cell.
A specialized junction where neurons use neurotransmitters to signal to other cells.
An electrically insulating phospholipid layer that surrounds the axons of many neurons.
The means by which action potentials are transmitted along myelinated fibers, jumping between nodes of Ranvier.
The first neurotransmitter identified; acts in both the peripheral and central nervous systems (e.g., at the neuromuscular junction).
An animal's reaction to a threat via a general discharge of the sympathetic nervous system.
